coredata.dc:title: Self-assembly of carbon quantum dots into C8-structured nanosheets
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Mary, K.A. Ann
Palakulam, Joyal Jain
Philip, Reji
coredata.dc:description: Among carbon family, development of carbon nanosheets through a green and simple method is a great challenge, owing to the ineffective synthetic approaches. Furthermore, though various carbon nanosheets are reported as highly efficient energy storage systems, other than graphitic carbon nitride, 2D carbon structures are seldom fluorescent. Here, C8 structured carbon nanosheets were synthesized from self-assembly of biomass derived carbon quantum dots. Their formation and assembly into non-graphitic crystalline 2D structures were investigated in detail with XRD spectra. While addition of PVP incited the formation of crystalline C8 structure, the presence of urea inhibited their growth. Moreover, the PVP could alter the morphology of nanosheets to nanoparticles with same cubic C8 structure. Both nanosheets and nanoparticles exhibited broad blue fluorescence similar to emission spectra exhibited by carbon quantum dots. The fluorescence intensity of C8 nanoparticles were selectively quenched after addition of Cu2+ ions and with a limit of detection of 103 nM. Phytotoxicity and cytotoxicity study of obtained nanosheets confirmed the potential behaviour of nanosheets for biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanosheets
Cubic C8
2D carbon
Crystalline carbon
Cu2+ sensor
Fluorescence sensing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are grateful to School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala, India and Centre for Nano and Soft Matter Sciences (CeNS), Bengaluru, Karnataka, India for providing the characterization facilities. We also thank Amala Cancer Research Centre, Thrissur, Kerala, India for their assistance in cytotoxicity tests and Central Instruments Laboratory, College of Veterinary & Animal Sciences, Mannuthy, Kerala, India for the help with lyophilization of samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction patterns of (a) prepared CDs, (b) freeze-dried CDs after rotary evaporation and (c) freeze-dried CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM, SAED and HRTEM image of DCD1 (a, c, e) and DCD2 (b, d, f). Inset of Figure b is histogram of particle size distribution and inset of Figure e & f is corresponding FFT image. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) FTIR of DCD1 and DCD2, EDX Spectra of (b) DCD1 (c) DCD2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Raman spectra of DCD2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
UV–Visible absorption spectra of (a) DCD1 and DCD2, (b) CD1 and CD2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) DCD1 (b) DCD2 obtained at different excitation wavelengths, Inset: CIE chromaticity diagram and photographic image under UV illumination (365 nm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Images of the PVA films from (a) DCD2 and (b) CD2 under daylight and UV excitations of 365 nm wavelength. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Fluorescence spectra of DCD2 in presence of varying concentrations of Cu2+ ions (a) and corresponding SV plot (b). Inset shows photographs of DCD2 solution under UV illumination (365 nm) for varying Cu2+ ions concentrations. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Cytotoxicity study of DCD2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic representation of preparation steps of carbon C8 structures.
Product yield of CD1, CD2 and CD3 prepared at different conditions.

head.ce:title: Self-assembly of carbon quantum dots into C8-structured nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Nanomaterials Research Division, Department of Physics, St. Thomas College, Thrissur, Kerala, 680001, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Nanomaterials Research Division, Department of Physics, St. Thomas College, Thrissur, Kerala, India. 680001

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ultrafast and Nonlinear Optics Lab, Light and Matter Physics Group, Raman Research Institute, Bengaluru, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ultrafast and Nonlinear Optics Lab, Light and Matter Physics Group, Raman Research Institute, Bengaluru, Karnataka, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Among carbon family, development of carbon nanosheets through a green and simple method is a great challenge, owing to the ineffective synthetic approaches. Furthermore, though various carbon nanosheets are reported as highly efficient energy storage systems, other than graphitic carbon nitride, 2D carbon structures are seldom fluorescent. Here, C8 structured carbon nanosheets were synthesized from self-assembly of biomass derived carbon quantum dots. Their formation and assembly into non-graphitic crystalline 2D structures were investigated in detail with XRD spectra. While addition of PVP incited the formation of crystalline C8 structure, the presence of urea inhibited their growth. Moreover, the PVP could alter the morphology of nanosheets to nanoparticles with same cubic C8 structure. Both nanosheets and nanoparticles exhibited broad blue fluorescence similar to emission spectra exhibited by carbon quantum dots. The fluorescence intensity of C8 nanoparticles were selectively quenched after addition of Cu2+ ions and with a limit of detection of 103 nM. Phytotoxicity and cytotoxicity study of obtained nanosheets confirmed the potential behaviour of nanosheets for biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon is the most abundant and crucial element whose tetravalency and catenation enables to form various allotropes such as fullerenes [1], three-dimensional bulk graphite and diamond, two-dimensional graphene and graphdiyne [2], one-dimensional carbon nanotubes [3] and zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CDs) [4]. 2D carbon materials recently attracted widespread attention due to their high thermal and electrical conductivity. Additionally, the large surface area and porosity marked them as a preferred choice for energy and environmental applications. However, the complex preparation routes of graphene involving toxic precursors can instigate health issues and environmental concerns. This ignited a surge of interest to expand this catalogue of metal free 2D materials through green and low-cost preparation methods. Not surprisingly, to maintain a green sustainable synthesis route and to reduce preparation cost, the strategy adopted is to make use of natural carbon sources. Tremendous efforts have been done on biomass derived carbon nanosheets including starch [5], waste coffee grounds [6], bacterial cellulose [7], hair [8], pineapple leaf [9], Leucaena leucocephala wood [10], spinach [11], cotton [12], citrus peels [13] etc. Although great progress has been achieved, to date, the promising method to prepare biomass derived 2D carbon materials is in situ carbonization and chemical activation processes using KOH [14]. Interestingly, similar to preparation of graphene nanosheets from unzipping carbon nanotubes [15], a new route can be developed to prepare 2D material from a 1D or 0D materials. Still, hardly no approaches focussed on direct bottom-up synthesis, where carbon nanosheets are developed from carbon quantum dots.

To date, efforts to synthesise 2D carbon results in synthesis of nanosheets which are graphitic in structure. Discovery of new carbon phases is a major challenge, but this formidable task can be achieved due to its ability to undergo broad sp, sp2 and sp3 hybridization. Regarding this issue, several strategies have been used to predict non graphitic crystalline phases of carbon. In 1978 Strel'nitskii et al. studied bcc-structured carbon allotrope named carbon-8 (C8) films [16]. Later, detailed analysis by Matyushenko NN et al. [17] found that the coordination number of C8 is 4 and mass density is around 4.1 g/cm3. The predictions on this “superdense carbon” [18] at high-pressure conditions says C–C bond length as 1.54 Å, which is almost the same as that in diamond [19]. Due to its near-diamond structural properties, there have been several successful attempts to synthesise C8 crystals, but hardly less experimental approaches could reveal carbon C8 structure. Recently, Blue-purple luminescent C8 carbon were studied for organic light-emitting device (OLEDs) applications [20–22]. However, their wide application is hindered by the complex synthesis, poor biocompatibilities and the hazardous precursor chemicals like EDTA or anthracene used are poisonous to living beings [23–25]. Although C8 structured CDs have been successfully obtained, the conclusions regarding their crystal structure are controversial and vivid. Experimental findings and detailed investigation on basic characterisations like X-Ray or electron diffraction studies are not readily available for a clear understanding of C8 structure. Contrastingly, due to lack of in-dept analysis, some of the carbon structures whose x-ray diffraction spectra looks similar to C8 structure are explained as graphitic or diamond lattice [26–31]. So, it is necessary to adopt new methodologies and suitable ‘natural’ sources for C8 carbon synthesis so that their application potential can be fully explored.

Commonly, carbon quantum dots are synthesized through carbonization process using organic compounds. Due to the abundant raw material availability, low cytotoxicity [32] and excellent optical properties [33], biomass derived CDs emerged as a perfect candidate for numerous applications [34–36]. The fluorescent CDs are mostly reported are either amorphous or graphitic in structure [37]. Though a general strategy adopted by researchers to dry CDs suspension into powder form is through freeze drying process, there were no reports yet either on formation of carbon nanosheets nor on crystalline C8 structure from graphitic CDs. Besides, freeze drying of CD suspension results in either amorphous or graphitic CD powders [38,39]. We executed a different approach where concentrated CDs are freeze-dried which resulted in formation new nongraphite 2D carbon material.

We executed a three-step synthesis method for preparation of carbon C8 structures as shown in Scheme 1 . 20 g of sun-dried Plectranthus amboinicus leaves were powdered and mixed well in a common 500 ml beaker with 300 mL of water under stirring. The beaker was then tightly covered using an aluminium foil sheet and paraffin wax paper and kept in the hot air oven at 95 °C for 24 h. The orange-brown coloured solution obtained was filtered using a filter membrane and named as CD1 suspension. The experiment was repeated in the presence of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) (6 g of PVP in 300 ml distilled water) and urea (21 g of urea in 300 ml distilled water). Black-brown solution obtained in the presence of PVP is named CD2 and urea is named CD3. In the next step, 90 ml of CD1 solution was taken in a round bottom flask and was subjected to rotary vacuum evaporation at an internal temperature of 32 °C for half an hour. The evaporation of solvent resulted in 10 ml of concentrated CD1 solution. This was kept in a cold trap at −120 °C for an hour and then in the freeze dryer/lyophilizer at −60 °C to −70 °C for 24 h. Brown-coloured powders obtained are named as DCD1, DCD2 and DCD3 corresponding to CD1, CD2 and CD3. The solid powder obtained from 10 ml of CD1, CD2 and CD3 solution through the same procedure of direct freeze-drying without rotary evaporation is named as FCD1, FCD2 and FCD3 respectively.

The XRD patterns of drop casted CD1, CD2, CD3 suspensions shown in Fig. 1 a exhibit characteristic broad peaks centred at 24° and 42° which can be attributed to the (002) and (100) planes of the hexagonal graphite structure [40]. The impurity peaks at 18.9°, 22.28°, 29.37° and 38.28° for the CD3 correspond to (002), (112), (003) and (004) planes of dopant urea [41]. The crystalline structure evolution of carbon C8 nanomaterials are elucidated from powder XRD (PXRD) spectra shown in Fig. 1b. The relative intensities of all diffraction peaks observed at 28.19°, 40.36°, 50.01°, 58.53°, 66.18° and 73.57° for DCD1 and DCD2 can be indexed to (011), (002), (112), (022), (013), and (222) planes of C8 structure (PDF card No. 72–2091). The average value of lattice parameter is obtained as 4.42 Ao for this polycrystalline structure with a bcc lattice. The lattice constant obtained lies in between the predicted values of 4.34 Ao for C8 microcrystals 4.89 Ao for supercubane structure [42]. It can be observed that peak positions of all diffraction peaks undergo a small shift to lower theta value which can be attributed to the uniform strain or macrostrain in the lattice due to intercalation of carbon. The inter planar spacings (dexp) obtained from the peak positions in XRD using Bragg's law is lower than the reference d-value from JCPDS. The macrostrain formed in DCD1 and DCD2 is evaluated using the relation:
M a c r o s t r a i n =
d −
d exp
d
and is shown in Fig. S1. It can be inferred that the negative strain is almost uniform for all the planes [43,44]. However, DCD3 shows same crystalline graphite nature of its parent CD3, exhibiting characteristic broad peak centred at 24° and 42° due to the (002) and (100) planes of graphitic structure. Here, the impurity peaks are removed after several times of washing with distilled water.

The formation of nanosheets (NS) can be studied from combining information obtained from XRD measurements in different ways [45]. While measuring PXRD pattern, all crystallographic planes in powder are aligned randomly which minimize any preferred orientation. X-ray diffraction patterns exhibit all possible planes orientations that exactly matches with the expected pattern for the bulk material. But when suspension of materials having 2D morphologies are drop casted, they will tend to orient with their flat faces parallel to the drying surface. The XRD spectrum of drop-casted DCD1 suspension (Fig. S2) exhibits enhanced intensity for diffraction peaks corresponding to the {hkl} crystal planes that are parallel to (110), including (220). This is due to a significant preferred orientation corresponding to (110) planes as most of NS lie parallel to the surface of the substrate.

Role of urea in inhibiting formation of carbon C
8
structure: Most of the previous works on carbon quantum dots synthesis involve carbonization process either in the presence of urea, ethylenediamine or any amine-based compounds [46]. Here, instead of using any amine-based precursors, an ingenious synthetic strategy was employed to synthesise carbon nanoparticles using PVP polymer. When carbon nanoparticles are prepared in the presence of urea (CD3), the crystalline carbon C8 structure is not obtained for DCD3, as evident from XRD spectrum (Fig. 1b). To confirm whether the presence of urea during the drying process inhibits the formation of the C8 structure in DCD1, rotary vacuum evaporation was performed after mixing 6.4 g of urea with 90 ml of CD1 which resulted in 10 ml of concentrate CD1 with urea (named as CD1U). This 10 ml of concentrated CD1U solution was then freeze-dried to obtain DCD1U. From Fig. S3, it can be inferred that the XRD spectrum does not exhibits diffraction peaks from crystalline carbon C8 planes, instead it shows diffraction peaks from the added urea. The presence of urea during drying process inhibits formation of crystalline carbon C8 structure. The novelty of our non-amine precursor-based synthesis route for carbon nanoparticles paved the way for the preparation of 2D crystalline C8 carbon. Moreover, despite the wide synthesis approaches reported on carbon quantum dots, the presence of amine-containing precursors or functional may inhibits the formation of C8 structure during freeze-drying process.

Growth and formation of carbon C
8
NS from condensed CDs: To unveil the growth mechanism, a more detailed investigation was done. The carbon nanosheets are formed during freeze drying process though orientated self-assembly of concentrated carbon quantum dots to form nanosheets. The interactions between π-π∗ orbitals along with dipole-dipole interaction between carbon layers and functionalized leads to stacking of CDs to form nanosheets [47–49]. Moreover, removal of solvent at low temperature causes the condensed suspension to shrunk and hence forcing the quantum dots to club together to form cubic C8 structure. The interplanar spacings of major graphitic crystalline planes (002) and (100) having values 0.335 Ao and 0.210 Ao are close to the prominent planes (110) and (200) of the C8 structure having d-values of 0.316 Ao and 0.223 Ao respectively. While freeze drying under reduced pressure and low temperature, these condensed graphitic crystallites undergo phase transition to C8 nanocrystallites with coherent interface formed by intersecting graphitic (002) – C8 (110) and graphitic (100) - C8 (200) planes. The XRD spectra of FCD1, FCD2 and FCD3 (Fig. 1c) clearly illustrate that crystalline non graphitic carbon C8 structure is only observed for FCD2. Without the prior process of rotary vacuum evaporation, the freeze drying of 10 ml of CD2 gives carbon C8 structure, while CD1 couldn't. From the product yield, 10 ml of CD1 does not contains critical concentration of CDs, while 10 ml of CD2 is more concentrated to form C8 structure upon freeze-drying process (Table 1 ). As discussed earlier, the presence of urea inhibits formation of crystalline C8 carbon form in FCD3.

Role of PVP: The role of PVP in the formation of CDs is well evident from the high product yield of DCD2. The presence of PVP promotes the faster nucleation and growth of CDs giving a concentrated CD2 solution compared to CD1 (CD2 was dark brown in colour than CD1). To examine more, 6 g of PVP was added to 90 ml CD1 solution, which is concentrated to 10 ml by rotary evaporation. After freeze drying this resultant solution, diffraction intensities from C8 structure are absent in XRD spectrum (Fig. S4) which suggests that PVP has no direct role in formation of crystalline C8 structure. Rather, CD2 could form crystalline C8 structure even upon freeze drying as the 10 ml CD2 solution already have critical concentration of CDs.

Nevertheless, formation of 2D-carbon C8 structure requires drying of condensed CDs at low temperature and pressure, not merely the freeze-drying drying of CDs or drying of condensed CDs. Moreover, on dilution of CD2 (2 ml in 8 ml of water), carbon C8 structure is not formed upon freeze-drying process as evident from Fig. S5. Here, yield was only 0.034 g ml−1. Additionally, when concentrated CDs (90 ml CD1 rotary evaporated to form 10 ml concentrated CDs) is dried at 60 °C in hot air oven for 2 h, crystalline C8 structure is not formed (Fig. S6). Thus, carbon C8 structure is formed if a critical concentration of CDs is allowed to condense and dry under low temperature and pressure.

The morphology and crystalline nature of DCD1 and DCD2 are examined from TEM and SAED images (Fig. 2 ). For DCD1, the NS have average lateral dimension of 414.6 nm. It is worth noting that NS have ripples and wrinkles. Fig. 2b reveals the formation of spherical nanoparticles (NP) for DCD2, having average size is in the range of 20–60 nm. Also, NP are homogeneously dispersed and spherical. The SAED pattern of both DCD1 and DCD2 given in Fig. 2c, d match well with the cubic carbon C8 structure, with prominent rings corresponding to diffraction peaks of (110), (200), (211), (220), (310) and (222) planes consistent with d-spacings as 0.316 Ao, 0.223 Ao, 0.184 Ao, 0.159 Ao, 0.140 Ao, 0.110 Ao respectively. This is well accordance with the XRD patterns obtained where all the diffraction rings observed are indexed to crystalline C8. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of DCD1 in Fig. 2e shows lattice planes with d-spacings determined as 0.315 nm corresponding to (110) plane of C8 carbon. The spherical morphology of nanoparticle is clear from the HRTEM image for DCD2 (Fig. 2f) and the size of the crystallites is in the range of 4–7 nm (Fig. S7). The interplanar spacings correspond to (110) planes of cubic carbon C8 structure. The presence of PVP has considerable influence in the morphology which led to the formation of homogeneous nanoparticles rather forming nanosheets. The TEM of CD1 reveals that the CDs have an average size in the range 9–14 nm which are aggregated together to form clusters (Fig. S8).

The FTIR spectra of DCD1 and DCD2 were measured and examined to unveil the presence of the functional groups and chemical bonds present in them (Fig. 3 ). The characteristic O–H stretching mode band is observed in the range 3011 cm−1 - 3556 cm−1 in Ref. [50]. While the small band in the range 2891 cm−1 - 2950 cm−1 indicates the presence of –CH stretching and bands in the range 2010 cm−1 -2150 cm−1 are due to –CH overtones. The broad absorption band peaked at 1550 cm−1 and 1631 cm−1 in DCD1 is due to the in-plane bending vibration of –NH bond and CO vibrations respectively. This band shifts to 1644 cm−1 in DCD2 due to the presence of CO stretching from PVP. Moreover, the bands in the range 1310 cm−1 – 1448 cm−1 peaked at 1409 cm−1 in DCD1 is the result of –CH and –CN bond vibrations while the peak shifts to 1423 cm−1 for DCD2 due to the presence of –CN stretching from PVP [51] The absorption band positioned at 1292 cm−1can be attributed to –CN stretching vibrations from PVP. The peak at 951 cm−1 is due to O–H bending in carboxylic acids and the small band in the region 1011 cm−1 -1070 cm−1 is due to C–O–C vibrations indicating presence of sp2 carbon atoms [51]. Moreover, 771 cm−1 corresponds to CH2 group stretching vibrations in nanosheets [52]. The EDX spectra of the DCD1 and DCD2 (Fig. 3b and c) gives the elemental composition of the dried carbon materials. Peaks confirming the energy levels of C, O, Cu, K, Mg, and P are present in the EDX spectra of both samples. Major peaks of oxygen in addition to carbon can be accredited to the presence of surface functional groups in NS and NP favouring the product with good solubility in water. Magnesium and potassium are two major minerals while phosphorus is a minor mineral in P. amboinicus extract [53].

The Raman spectrum of carbon C8 nanoparticles, DCD2 is shown in Fig. 4 . The G-band (sp2 hybridization) and D-band (sp3 hybridization) of graphitic carbon prepared from other natural carbon sources have peaks around 1350 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 respectively [54,55]. Obtained carbon C8 exhibits two broad peaks at 1336.9 cm−1 and 1573.7 cm−1 which are close to the D (disorder) and G (graphite) bands, respectively due to the crystallographic similarity between C8 and graphitic structure. Vibration of sp2 hybridised carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice contributes to intensity of the G-band [56]. Since the G-band is higher in intensity compared to D-band, there is a higher degree of graphitization of the sample which is evident from a relatively lower ID/IG ratio of 0.714. Khanam et al. has used Raman spectra to determine grain size of carbon nanoparticles based on the formula [57]:
G r a i n
S i z e =
44
{
I D
I G
}
(
in
A o
)
According to this equation, the grain size of C8 sample DCD2 is 6 nm.

UV–Visible absorption spectra of carbon nanosheets and carbon nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 5 a. The absorption characteristics of both remains same in the visible region while slight increase in absorption around 310 nm and 279 nm is observed for carbon nanosheets. The absorption band at 279 nm for is due to π-π∗ transition of the sp2 hybridised CC bond in the carbonaceous core. Theoretical predictions on C8 structure suggest that it must have a strong absorption at 230–270 nm [58]. The spectral properties of cubic C8 carbon nanosheets and nanoparticles are similar with their corresponding graphitic CDs solutions from which they are prepared (Fig. 5b). Absorption band located between 265 and 283 nm for CD1 and band peaked at 280 nm for CD2 can be attributed to interlayer transition of oxygen functionalized graphitic core or the π-π∗ bonding - antibonding orbital transition of the sp2 hybridised CC bond [59]. Broad absorption after 325 nm is due to the transition between the nonbonding orbital of edge groups to π∗ orbital at the boundary of the sp2 and sp3 hybridised carbon. It is due to the contributions from surface groups and their δ-π∗ transitions [60]. The change in crystal structure and morphology doesn't have much effect on optical absorption of carbon nanomaterials.

PL spectra of DCD1 and DCD2 are shown in Fig. 6 . Corresponding CIE diagram shows that emission colour spans from blue to green with increase in excitation wavelengths. The C8 carbon nanosheets exhibits same spectral properties of their graphitic CDs solutions (Fig. S9), while most of the carbon sheets reported are rarely fluorescent. The fluorescence observed upon excitation of 360 nm LED is shown in the inset of Fig. 6. Carbon NS prepared from self-assembly of carbon CDs retains the same spectral properties of the building blocks. Functional groups like –OH, –COOH, –CO attached randomly to the surface of the NS act as multi-chromophores in them. The excitation spectra measured at emission wavelengths 459 nm for DCD1 and 449 nm for DCD2 is shown in Fig. S10.

As most of the carbon quantum dots suffer from serious self-quenching in the solid state, DCD1 and DCD2 was not emissive in solid state. This is caused by π–π stacking or excessive nonradiative energy transfer as they aggregate themselves. To avoid this aggregation caused quenching, DCD2 is dispersed in a polymer matrix. The NP and its corresponding CDs are excellent fluorescent solid materials, when they were embedded in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix. The PVA films observed under daylight and under UV excitation of 360 nm wavelength is shown in Fig. 7 .

The fluorescence response of DCD2 solution is used for selective sensing of Cu2+ ions. The fluorescence response of C8 NP solution in the presence of different metal ions (5 mM) is recorded and emission intensity at 370 nm wavelength is plotted (Fig. S10). Upon addition of Cu2+ ions, significant quenching in the blue emission of the solution is observed compared to other metal ions.

The strong blue emission of the C8 nanosheet solution is quenched on adding different doses of Cu2+ ions as shown in Fig. 8 a. From the FTIR studies, it is clear that DCD2 contains different oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups. Additionally, presence of CO groups at nanoparticle surfaces due to the presence of PVP pave the way towards the strong interaction of the DCD2 with the Cu2+ ions [61].

To calculate the limit of detection (LOD), the graph is plotted between intensity variation [I0/I] and different doses of Cu2+ ions (Fig. 8b). The Stern Volmer (SV) plot exhibits a linear response to the Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0–30 μM. The Stern - Volmer curve can be depicted as
I0/I = 1.0168 + 0.00773x (R2 = 0.985)
where ‘x’ is the concentration of Cu2+ in the micromolar (μM) range with a correlation coefficient of 0.985. The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 103 nM which is below the upper limit of copper ions that can be present in the drinking water (20 μM) according to the U.S. EPA [62]. It can be seen that the efficiency of carbon C8 nanoparticles is remarkable when compared to several CDs derived from chemical precursors through complex reaction steps. To evaluate the practical applicability, tap water samples are collected from a copper tap in our laboratory and analysed with the developed fluorescent probe. The sample was filtered using Whatman filter paper (∼110 μm thickness) prior to the detection. 3 ml aliquots of tap water containing carbon nanoparticles is spiked with standard Cu2+ solution (0–1 mM). From the fluorescence measurements done, recovery is [63]. The sensor developed provides a linear response (R2 = 0.9937) to the copper ions concentrations and shows recovery rates of 116 %, 92 % and 101 % for 0.5 mM, 1 mM and 2 mM concentrations of spiked samples respectively. This demonstrates that the assay is good for real sample detection and validates capability of using the sensor in environmental applications.

To examine the biocompatibility nature of carbon C8 nanoparticles, cell viability test was performed on rat spleen cells (RSC) by trypan blue exclusion method [64]. On counting the numbers of stained and unstained cells separately, the percentage of living cells (Cell viability) can be determined (Fig. 9 ). Low cell toxicity levels examined even after addition of higher concentration of DCD2 illustrates that carbon C8 structure is perfect candidate for biosensing and bioimaging applications. In recent decades luminescent carbon quantum dots have been extensively applied in bioscience for biosensing and bioimaging, but the influence of carbon nanoparticles on plants has been hardly investigated.

To extent the possibility of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials for live imaging in plant system, the effect of nanoparticles on seed germination and plant growth are to be investigated. The dose-response study of carbon C8 nanoparticles on the phytotoxicity of mung bean plants are done as shown in Table S1. From our observations, it is clear that DCD2 is phyto-friendly as the seed germination started with excellent results on the first day itself. By the end of the second day all the seeds in petri dishes were germinated where some of them even started growing leaves and stems.

In this work, a novel strategy is developed to prepare cubic C8 carbon nanosheets through a simple, eco-friendly, reliable and easy method. Large quantities of CDs were easily prepared through the pyrolysis of withered leaves. Crystalline carbon C8 nanosheets are formed after freeze drying of concentrated graphitic CDs after rotatory evaporation. With the addition of PVP, the morphology changes from nanosheets to nanoparticles. PVP acts as a growth modifier as formation of NS are hindered due to the presence of long alkyl chains. Additionally, PVP acts as a catalyst to increase the product yield of CDs and hence to result in formation of crystalline C8 structure upon freeze drying. Furthermore, we found that either the addition of urea during synthesis of CDs or presence of urea during freeze drying inhibits the formation of crystalline C8 structure. On the other hand, the optical properties remain unaltered for graphitic CDs and crystalline C8 structure nanosheets. Upon wide range of excitations, both NP and NS exhibits excitation dependent emissions from blue to green, like emissions observed from corresponding CDs. This is retained in solid PVA matrix. The blue emission of C8 carbon nanoparticles is exploited to design fluorescent probe for Cu2+ ions with a detection limit of 103 nM. The experimental results shows that nanostructures have nontoxic nature. The blue emission of PVP-C8 nanoparticles is exploited to design fluorescent prob for Cu2+ ions. The current work made a significant contribution towards preparation of non-graphitic carbon nanosheets with cubic C8 structure.

K.A. Ann Mary: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Joyal Jain Palakulam: Writing – original draft, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation. Reji Philip: Validation, Project administration, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala, India and Centre for Nano and Soft Matter Sciences (CeNS), Bengaluru, Karnataka, India for providing the characterization facilities. We also thank Amala Cancer Research Centre, Thrissur, Kerala, India for their assistance in cytotoxicity tests and Central Instruments Laboratory, College of Veterinary & Animal Sciences, Mannuthy, Kerala, India for the help with lyophilization of samples.

The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2025.119991.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: C60: buckminsterfullerene
Nature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. W. Kroto, J. R. Heath, S. C. O’Brien, R. F. Curl, and R. E. Smalley, “C60: Buckminsterfullerene,” Nature, vol. 318, pp. 162-163, 1985.

Zhong, “New candidate for the simple cubic carbon sample shock-synthesized by compression of the mixture of carbon black and tetracyanoethylene,” Carbon N Y, vol. 112, pp. 91-96, Feb. 2017, doi: 10.1016/j.carbon.2016.11.008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolour fluorescent carbon nanoparticle probes for live cell imaging and dual palladium and mercury sensors
J. Mater. Chem. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Sharma, A. K. Saini, and S. M. Mobin, “Multicolour fluorescent carbon nanoparticle probes for live cell imaging and dual palladium and mercury sensors,” J Mater Chem B, vol. 4, no. 14, pp. 2466-2476, Apr. 2016, doi: 10.1039/c6tb00238b.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Warm to cool tunable ultra-stable white light emissions from carbon dots -Tb3+ - Eu3+ doped silica
Opt Mater (Amst)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Paul, J. J. Palakulam, N. V. Unnikrishnan, R. Philip, and K. A. A. Mary, “Warm to cool tunable ultra-stable white light emissions from carbon dots -Tb3+ - Eu3+ doped silica,” Opt Mater (Amst), vol. 138, Apr. 2023, doi: 10.1016/j.optmat.2023.113673.
